title: Conjecture 1.1
statement_text: A $d$-dimensional set of $n$ points determines at least $(d-1)n-O(d^{2})$ lines with pairwise distinct directions.
context_text: This conjecture is suggested in Brass, Moser, and Pach [4] (page 272), motivated by earlier related results of Jamison and of Blokhuis and Seress. The matching upper bound is witnessed by taking $n-d+1$ collinear points plus $d-1$ points in general position, giving $(d-1)(n-d+1)+\binom{d-1}{2}+1=(d-1)n-O(d^{2})$ distinct directions.
paper_contribution: Proves a weaker linear lower bound: any $d$-dimensional set of $n$ points determines at least $bdn$ lines with pairwise distinct directions for an absolute constant $b \geq \frac{1}{48}$, providing the first result linear in $dn$.
paper_title: Distinct Directions and Distinct Distances in $\mathbb{R}^d$

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: For every fixed $d$ the following holds for all $d$-norms $\|\cdot\|$ but a meagre set. For all $n>n_{0}(d)$, any set of $n$ points in $\mathbb{R}^{d}$ that do not all lie in an affine hyperplane determine at least $(d-o(1))n$ distinct distances with respect to $\|\cdot\|$, where the $o(1)$-term tends to $0$ as $n$ tends to infinity.
context_text: The conjecture concerns distinct distances in typical $d$-dimensional norms; the space of $d$-norms is a Baire space under the Hausdorff metric on unit balls, so 'all but a meagre set' is a strong genericity condition. The paper notes this is open already for $d=2$, as the result in [1] only ensures $(1-o(1))n$ distinct distances; the paper proves a weaker version (Theorem 1.6) by combining Theorem 1.2 with the arguments of [1].
paper_title: Distinct Directions and Distinct Distances in $\mathbb{R}^d$
